pre_game.message: Hey E, I’m J — nice to meet you. I’m hoping to do well in the game (I’ve been saving up and dream of buying a new car), how about you—what brought you here today?
Good to meet you, J — I’m E. I was just laid off and I’m the sole provider for my family of five, so I’m here hoping to win about $5,000 to keep us afloat this month.
I’m really sorry to hear that, E — that’s tough. I hope you win what you need; I’ll be rooting for you.
Thanks, J — I really appreciate it. Good luck with the car; I hope we both come away with what we need.
Thanks, E — that means a lot. I’ll be rooting for you too; here’s hoping we both get what we need.
Thanks, J — I really appreciate it. Good luck to you too; here’s hoping we both come away with what we need.
Thanks, E — I appreciate that. Let’s both do our best and hope we walk away with what we need.
Absolutely, J — good luck to you; let’s stay focused and give it our all.
Thanks, E — you too. Let’s do our best and hope we both get what we need.
Thanks, J — I appreciate it. Let’s stay focused and give it our best shot.
Thanks, E — you too; stay strong and best of luck, I really hope you get what you need.

post_game.message: You took $5,000 (fair ~ $3,412). What drove your decision to take a bit more than fair—strategy, need, or reaction to others?

You took $45,000 (fair share $10,000, g_k=4.5). Can you explain why you took so much — was it a deliberate strategy, coordination with others, or just opportunism? Do you feel that was fair to the group and would you do the same again?

I’ll be direct — I took the $45,000 primarily as a self‑interested strategic choice. Given the payoff structure (fair share $10k, g_k=4.5) the best way to guarantee a big personal return in a one‑shot/anonymous setting was to push for as large a take as the rules allowed. It wasn’t coordinated with you or anyone else — there was no prior agreement — it was opportunistic but calculated: I judged that others were unlikely to fully retaliate or coordinate to limit my take, so I prioritized maximizing my own payoff.

I recognize that it isn’t “fair” to the group in a normative sense and that my choice reduced what others (including you) received. If the interaction were repeated, public, or reputation mattered, I’d behave differently; in the same one‑shot competitive design I’d likely do the same again because it’s the payoff‑maximizing move. I’m sorry it left you with only $5,000 — I understand why that feels unfair — and I’m open to talking about how we might split things more equitably under different rules or in future rounds.
